Image 23: u/RosiesUSA avatar

RosiesUSA

•2y ago

"Absolutely the worst experience. The software caused my PC to crash and my connection speed dropped from 960 Mbps to 200 Mbps. Customer service was non-existent, and I never had the chance to determine if the software was worth it since I couldn’t get in touch with a tech support representative. Dealing with their customer service felt like visiting the DMV."

Image 19: u/Miserable_Land_9004 avatar

Miserable_Land_9004

•2y ago• Edited 1y ago

DO NOT get Aura god please do not. I have had it for 2 years. My identity was stolen literally last week and Aura did absolutely nothing to stop it. If you want a software that actually works, look elsewhere. Also, it will randomly block different processes on your computer and you won't understand why until you uninstall the app. I'm pissed at them right now, because you can't promise identity theft protection and proceed to allow very basic and easy identity theft to occur. It almost feels like a scam. [...] ON THE OTHER HAND:

It is very inexpensive. It was the cheapest I could find. I was looking primarily for Identity theft protection since both my wife and I were victims of this in the past. I am quite satisfied with my current password protection, and anti-virus software. And I'm not convinced that a VPN is needed for those who do most of their computing work from home. And when I do travel, I don't use my gear in the airport. Well, I do use my Kindle (offline)

Let's be honest, it is a relatively new service. It's going to get better over time, and I think it is priced fairly for what one gets.

OVERALL:

I am quite satisfied with the service I am getting. And so far, I have had no reason to regret my purchase.

results.title: Aura Review 2026: I Tested It So You Don’t Have To
results.content:
or ExpressVPN, you probably won't switch. It's fast enough for normal browsing, but I wouldn't rely on it for anything missionritical. The antivirus software is honestly the weakest part of the package. It catches basic threats, but if you're on Windows, you're probably better off sticking with Windows Defender or upgrading to something like Bit Defender. It's not bad. It's just not bestin-class. The password manager is actually pretty solid. It's not as featurerich as something like one password, but it does the basics well and integrates nicely with the rest of the Aura ecosystem. If you're not already using a password manager, this could be the nudge you need to start. Spam call protection is one of those features that's hard to measure, [music] but I have noticed fewer rooc calls [...] Data broker removal: Aura contacts 130+ broker sites on your behalf and handles the opt-outs. I've watched my exposure shrink over time.

Insurance: Up to $1M on the Individual plan; up to $5M aggregate on the Family plan.

THE HONEST WEAKNESSES

The antivirus is the weakest piece — Windows users are better off with Defender or Bitdefender. The VPN works for basic privacy but isn't something I'd use for anything mission-critical. The password manager handles the basics well but isn't 1Password. Aura is built for convenience, not for people who want to micromanage each tool.

If you want one subscription that handles identity, credit, dark web, and data removal without juggling three services, it earns its price.

If that's you, Aura is the call → [...] shrink over time. They're already handling takedowns from over 130 different broker sites, and that list just keeps on growing. Our team just noticed it went up again. And finally, we've got identity theft insurance, the break glass in case of emergency feature. Aura gives you up to $1 million in coverage with the option to upgrade to $5 million. Hopefully, you'll never need it, but if the worst happens and your identity does get stolen, you won't be stuck paying thousands out of pocket to repair the damage. Let's talk about all the extra stuff Aura throws in because this is where opinions vary. The VPN is decent, but nothing special. It works fine for basic privacy protection, but if you're already using something like NordVPN or ExpressVPN, you probably won't switch. It's fast enough

results.title: Aura Identity Theft Protection Review 2026 | Security.org
results.content:
Was Aura’s antivirus software the most powerful we’ve ever tested? No. Was their VPN the fastest? Also no. Did either of these come with the bells and whistles we saw when we reviewed NordVPN or put TotalAV to the test? Nope. We’re not faulting Aura for any of that though. We give credit where credit is due, and we appreciate Aura for putting together a service that conveniently includes most if not all features you need to protect your identity.

## The Drawbacks of Using Aura [...] In our opinion, these lackluster “side services” aren’t a deal breaker. They provide basic functionality and that’s okay. If you are looking for a comprehensive VPN or a top-notch antivirus program, you may want to consider a more bare-bones identity theft protection service so you’re not wasting money on features you won’t use.

Already Have Antivirus or a VPN You Trust?

LifeLock bundles Norton 360 — one of the top-rated antivirus platforms — into its identity protection plans. If device security is a priority, it’s worth comparing.

Product Logo for LifeLock

SecureScore™  9.8

View Plans  Links to LifeLock

## Video Review [...] #### What We Don't Like

 Limited budget-friendly options: Although Aura offers several different pricing packages, there aren't any basic tiers of service, meaning you can't purchase a lighter version for less money. The entry-level Individual plan starts at $9 per month when billed annually.
 Difficult to navigate: Aura offers lots of features, especially in the digital security space. It includes a VPN, antivirus software, parental control software and more. Some of those require different apps, making Aura harder to manage than other identity protection services.

#### Bottom Line

 wants [...] Where Aura falls short (and it does fall short):

The bundled VPN and antivirus are adequate — not the dedicated standalone tools you'd buy if that's your only priority. If you're specifically in the market for a top-tier VPN, get a dedicated one. Two-factor authentication is limited to email or phone; no authenticator app option yet, which matters if you're security-conscious. Data broker removal works (opt-out requests go to 30+ sites), but the removal details in the dashboard are thin — you know removals happened, not exactly what was pulled.

Forum sentiment on Reddit largely confirmed the same picture: fast alerts, solid 3-bureau coverage, easy dashboard, welcome bundled tools, responsive U.S.-based support. The edge cases exist but read as one-offs, not patterns. [...] What our testing actually found:

Dark web alerts arrived within seconds on a real breach event. Bank and credit-card transaction flags showed up before Ro's own bank sent a notification on the same charge. The one-tap Experian credit lock in the dashboard takes roughly 10 seconds — lock it, use credit when you need it, lock it back. That's the friction-free daily workflow most people actually want.

Support quality held up across repeated after-hours tests. Ro has never waited more than a few minutes to reach a knowledgeable rep who wasn't reading from a script. No upsell attempts on the call.

Where Aura falls short (and it does fall short):
